critic_raw:
The unrolled network for phase retrieval learns a reference image that is claimed to generalize to unseen data distributions. This generalization claim has a structural problem: the reference is learned end-to-end through backpropagation on training data, meaning its optimal form is tuned to the statistics of the training distribution. An unrolled network has a fixed number of iterations baked into its architecture, so its error trajectory is calibrated to the training distribution's convergence dynamics. When applied to an unseen distribution with different spectral characteristics or spatial statistics, the fixed iteration count may be either too few (non-convergence) or too many (divergence toward noise), and the reference that minimized training loss will not be the reference that minimizes recovery error on out-of-distribution signals.

The robustness claim to spatial augmentations (shifting and rotation) in phase retrieval has a mathematical subtlety: a spatial shift in the image domain corresponds to a linear phase ramp in the Fourier domain. Since phase retrieval discards phase information, a shifted version of a signal produces a different magnitude spectrum, not a trivially equivalent one. The claim that the learned reference is 'robust to shifting' therefore requires either that the network learns a reference insensitive to linear phase ramps (a non-trivial architectural requirement) or that the claim is being evaluated only on small shifts where the magnitude difference is negligible. The mechanism is not established.

Validation on 'open-access datasets' without specifying whether these datasets have the same underlying signal structure as the training data is insufficient to support the distribution generalization claim. If the open-access datasets share acquisition parameters (resolution, noise model, imaging modality) with the training data, the experiment tests interpolation, not extrapolation.

The small-data regime claim (learned on 'a small amount of data') is not quantified in the description. Without knowing what 'small' means relative to the signal dimensionality, the claim is vacuous — a reference that is essentially a mean image learned from 50 samples may perform well on in-distribution data simply because the mean is close to the ground truth for that distribution.
all_issues_raised: The reference image is learned end-to-end on training data, so its optimal form is distribution-specific; the fixed iteration count of the unrolled network calibrates convergence to training-data spectral statistics, undermining generalization claims to unseen distributions.

The robustness-to-shifting claim has a mathematical inconsistency: spatial shifts produce linear phase ramps and therefore different magnitude spectra, so 'robustness to shifting' requires either magnitude-insensitive reference design or restricts to negligibly small shifts — neither is established.

Validation on 'open-access datasets' does not establish out-of-distribution generalization unless the datasets differ meaningfully in acquisition parameters and signal statistics from the training set.

The 'small amount of data' claim is unquantified relative to signal dimensionality, making the sample-efficiency claim unverifiable and potentially trivial if the reference degenerates to a distribution mean.
all_issues_adjudicated: The reference image is learned end-to-end on training data, so its optimal form is distribution-specific; the fixed iteration count of the unrolled network calibrates convergence to training-data spectral statistics, undermining generalization claims to unseen distributions.

The robustness-to-shifting claim has a mathematical inconsistency: spatial shifts produce linear phase ramps and therefore different magnitude spectra, so 'robustness to shifting' requires either magnitude-insensitive reference design or restricts to negligibly small shifts — neither is established.

Validation on 'open-access datasets' does not establish out-of-distribution generalization unless the datasets differ meaningfully in acquisition parameters and signal statistics from the training set.

The 'small amount of data' claim is unquantified relative to signal dimensionality, making the sample-efficiency claim unverifiable and potentially trivial if the reference degenerates to a distribution mean.
